meta.name: Olive Oil Focaccia
meta.subtitle: Crisp edges • airy crumb • dimpled top • rosemary + flaky salt • fresh milled or commercial flour

source.notes: High-hydration focaccia with olive oil-rich pan method. Includes same-day and cold-ferment options, plus fresh-milled guidance and topping variations.

yield.notes: One 9x13-inch focaccia, cut into ~12 squares (adjust as desired).

tasteProfile.notes: Olive oil aroma, salty crust, and tender open crumb. Best eaten warm or toasted.

ingredients.label: Flour (bread flour preferred; or all-purpose; or fresh milled wheat flour—see Fresh-Milled Notes)
ingredients.notes: Bread flour improves structure at high hydration. All-purpose works; crumb may be slightly less open.

ingredients.label: Water, lukewarm (80–95°F / 27–35°C)
ingredients.notes: High hydration (80%). If using fresh milled, you may need to adjust slightly.

ingredients.label: Instant yeast (or active dry yeast)
ingredients.notes: About 1 1/2 teaspoons. If using active dry, bloom in part of the water first.

ingredients.label: Extra-virgin olive oil (for pan and top)
ingredients.notes: About 1/4 cup. Don’t skimp—oil is key to crisp edges and flavor.

ingredients.label: Rosemary, fresh (or dried)
ingredients.notes: About 2 tablespoons chopped fresh, or 2 teaspoons dried.

ingredients.label: Flaky salt (for topping)
ingredients.notes: Use to taste; start light and add more after baking if needed.

steps.title: Mix shaggy dough
instructions: In a large bowl, whisk flour and yeast together (if using instant).
Add water and mix until no dry flour remains.
Cover and rest 10 minutes (brief hydration rest helps dough smooth out).

steps.title: Add salt and olive oil, then fold
instructions: Sprinkle salt over the dough; drizzle in olive oil (in-dough amount).
Pinch and fold the dough until salt and oil are incorporated. Dough will look wet and uneven at first—this is normal.
Do 2–3 minutes of folding or gentle mixing until more cohesive.

steps.title: Strengthen dough with stretch-and-folds
instructions: Over the next 60–90 minutes, perform 3 sets of stretch-and-folds (or coil folds) spaced 20–30 minutes apart.
Each set: lift one side, stretch gently, fold over; rotate bowl and repeat 4 times.
After the final fold, the dough should feel more elastic and hold shape slightly.

steps.title: Bulk rise until puffy
instructions: Cover and let dough rise at room temperature until visibly puffy and increased in volume (about 1.5–2x), 60–90 minutes.
If your kitchen is cool, it may take longer.

steps.title: Oil pan and move dough
instructions: Pour about half of the pan/top olive oil into a 9x13 pan and tilt to coat bottom and sides well.
Scrape dough into the pan. Flip it once so both sides get a thin oil coat.
Gently press dough toward the corners; if it resists, rest 10 minutes and try again.

steps.title: Final proof in pan
instructions: Cover and proof until dough is very airy, jiggly, and nearly fills the pan, 45–75 minutes.
You should see bubbles under the surface.

steps.title: Dimple and top
instructions: Heat oven to 425°F (220°C).
Drizzle remaining olive oil over the dough.
Oil your fingertips and press deep dimples all over the surface (go nearly to the bottom of the pan).
Add rosemary and/or other toppings (tomatoes, olives, onions, garlic). Finish with flaky salt.

steps.title: Bake until deep golden
instructions: Bake 22–28 minutes until deep golden brown with crisp edges.
Internal temp target: ~200–205°F (93–96°C).
If top colors too fast, reduce to 400°F (205°C) for the last 5–8 minutes.

steps.title: Cool briefly, then serve
instructions: Cool 10–15 minutes in the pan for structure.
For crispest bottom, lift focaccia out and cool on a rack another 10 minutes before slicing.

freshMilledNotes.summary: Fresh milled flour can produce excellent focaccia, though bran can slightly tighten crumb. Adjust hydration and allow extra rest for best openness.
guidelines: Best balance: 70% fresh milled flour + 30% bread flour (same total flour weight).
If using 100% fresh milled (unsifted), consider sifting out some bran for a lighter crumb.
Fresh milled may absorb more water—add +10–25g water if dough feels stiff after Step 2.
A longer bulk rise or a cold ferment can improve flavor and extensibility with fresh milled flour.

variations.name: Cold-ferment focaccia (best flavor)
variations.change: After Step 3 (folds), cover and refrigerate 12–48 hours. Bring to room temp 60–90 minutes, then proceed with pan step and proof.
variations.notes: More complex flavor and improved dough strength.

variations.name: Chewy, thicker focaccia
variations.change: Use a smaller pan (9x9) or a 10-inch cast iron skillet for a thicker focaccia; bake 2–5 minutes longer.

variations.name: Focaccia sandwich slab
variations.change: Bake plain (only salt), then slice horizontally for sandwiches.
variations.notes: Great for pressed sandwiches; toasts well.

variations.name: Onion + olive focaccia
variations.change: Top with thin red onions and olives; finish with rosemary and flaky salt.

storage.counter: Best day-of. Store airtight up to 2 days.
storage.freezer: Freeze slices up to 2 months; reheat/toast from frozen.
storage.reheat: Best: toast or warm in a 375°F (190°C) oven for 6–10 minutes to re-crisp edges.

notes: The dough should be very wet—resist adding flour; folding builds strength over time.
Deep dimples help create the classic focaccia texture and keep toppings in place.
Olive oil in the pan is what creates the signature crisp, almost fried bottom edges.

audit.testNotes: Schema-compliant: schemaVersion+meta present; ingredients include id+label+amount; steps include id+title+instructions.
